---
title: Resampling Methods
slug: resampling-methods
---

## Why Resample?

Classical inference leans on closed-form sampling distributions that exist only
for a handful of statistics under strong assumptions. Resampling replaces those
assumptions with computation: we treat the sample as a stand-in for the
population and re-draw from it. For onboarding, <BookLink book="handbook" to="/chapters/prompting">the day-to-day workflow</BookLink> is the gentler place to begin before this chapter.

The plug-in principle underlies everything here: to estimate a feature of a
distribution, compute that same feature of the empirical distribution. Its
validity is guaranteed by the central limit theorem <XRef id="thm-clt"/> in
the regimes where the statistic is asymptotically normal.

## The Nonparametric Bootstrap

The bootstrap was introduced by Efron in 1979 <Cite key="efron1979"/> as a
computational stand-in for the delta method. A single bootstrap replicate draws
n observations with replacement from the original n; the resampling loop in
<CodeRef path="src/resample.py" line={30} lineEnd={70}/> shows the inner draw.

Computing the statistic across many replicates yields an empirical sampling
distribution. The percentile interval reads its 2.5th and 97.5th quantiles
directly. The standard reference text is <Cite key="efrontib1993"/>, which
remains the clearest introduction for practitioners.

Because the empirical distribution function converges uniformly to the truth
<XRef id="thm-glivenko-cantelli"/>, the resampled quantiles converge to the
population quantiles as n grows.

## Bias Correction

A plug-in estimator is often biased. The bootstrap estimates that bias by
comparing the mean of the replicate statistics to the statistic on the full
sample. This machinery builds directly on the ordinary nonparametric bootstrap of
<XRef id="sec-bootstrap-basics"/>, adding one layer of correction.

The corrected estimate subtracts the bootstrap bias estimate from the original.
For the percentile helper in <CodeRef path="src/intervals.py" line={12} lineEnd={30}/>
we apply the same shift before reading the quantiles.

## BCa Intervals

The percentile interval is transformation-respecting but not second-order
accurate. The bias-corrected and accelerated interval refines <XRef id="sec-bootstrap-bias"/>
by adding an acceleration constant estimated from the influence function.

The accelerated variant is due to Efron (1987) <Cite key="efron1987"/>, who showed it achieves
second-order accuracy under smoothness. When correcting for median bias, discussed under
<XRef id="sec-bootstrap-bias"/>, the acceleration term vanishes and BCa reduces
to the simpler bias-corrected interval.

## The Jackknife

The jackknife predates the bootstrap. It estimates variance by leaving out one
observation at a time and measuring how much the statistic moves. Each
leave-one-out replicate is deterministic, so the jackknife needs no random
number generator at all.

The jackknife is a linear approximation to the bootstrap and can fail badly for
non-smooth statistics such as the median.

## Permutation Tests

Unlike the jackknife discussed in <XRef id="sec-jackknife"/>, which removes
one observation at a time, a permutation test resamples labels without replacement
to test a hypothesis. The core idea is shuffling labels to build a null distribution
against which the observed statistic is compared.

Permutation p-values are exact under the null of exchangeability, which is why
they are preferred when that assumption is defensible.

## When Resampling Fails

Every method here assumes the sample captures the population's tail behavior, and
the permutation test described in <XRef id="sec-permutation"/> further leans on
exchangeability. Under heavy tails or strong dependence the resampled distribution
misleads, and no amount of computation repairs a sample that never saw the extremes.
